segments.title: Constellation cancellation and US frigate roles
segments.question: What are your thoughts on the announced cancellation of further Constellation-class builds?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke says the program suffered because the US Navy wanted a task-force frigate rather than a simple convoy escort, while requirements and procurement politics kept shifting.
segments.summary: A Bruships answer uses the Constellation cancellation to explain why US frigate requirements, carrier-group support, convoy assumptions, and procurement discipline all matter.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that the Constellation problem starts with mission clarity: the US Navy was looking for a ship to support task forces and carrier battle groups, not a straightforward convoy escort. He then links that role to a China scenario, where Pacific operations would be shaped by island-chain pressure, limited allied convoying, and the need to plan for more than a short war. The answer closes by putting blame on moving requirements and procurement politics, warning that a new unmanned or blank-sheet replacement would still have to solve presence, integration, and production problems.

evidence.note: The question asks about cancellation of further Constellation-class builds, and the answer distinguishes a task-force frigate from a convoy escort.
The China-scenario discussion frames convoying, island-chain operations, allied escorts, and the danger of assuming a short war.
The procurement section says the US Navy had people managing procurement without a settled view of what they wanted and points to changing requirements.
The follow-on answer says moving goalposts hurt procurement and explains why unmanned or blank-sheet designs would still struggle with frigate presence missions.
